outline_json.title: The Scribe's Quiet Lie

scenes.description: Maret's hand clenches a quill too tightly, snapping the nib. She quickly conceals the broken pieces and mutters about cheap supplies, hiding her unnatural strength from nearby scribes.
Theo Marlow sits beside her, noticing her stiffness. She deflects with work talk, but he mentions rumors of a System Arbiter in the city investigating some data corruption.
Maret internally panics at the news. She calls up her System interface—Strength: 3. She knows it's a lie and remembers bending an iron bar last week. She clenches her fist, conflicted.

Guild Master Elara summons Maret to her office and warns her that an Arbiter has requested access to guild records to investigate an anomaly in the Scribal Quarter. Elara advises her to stay out of trouble.
Needing to confirm her hidden strength, Maret goes to a storage room, picks up a steel ruler, and bends it easily. Her ability is real—she hasn't lost it.
Theo returns to her desk and asks where she went. She lies about fetching supplies. He offers to help if she's in trouble, but she firmly declines.
A junior scribe delivers a notice: the Arbiter will visit the guild tomorrow to interview all senior scribes. Maret's anxiety spikes as her secret closes in.
Maret considers fleeing but realizes it would confirm guilt. She resolves to stay and maintain her story. She mentally rehearses her scribal role, preparing a defensive lie.
At the end of the day, Maret leaves the guild. She spots a figure in Arbiter robes across the street and freezes—but he turns away, just a local Arbiter, not Cassel. She exhales and walks home.
Alone in her room, Maret studies her hands and remembers the first time she bent iron at age sixteen. The memory both frightens and steadies her. She vows silently not to be caught.

outline_json.purpose: Establishes Maret's hidden strength and her carefully maintained facade of weakness, while introducing the arrival of Arbiter Cassel in the city, ending with the news that he is investigating an anomaly in the Scribal Quarter.

establishedFacts.fact: Maret's Strength stat reads 3, but she is far stronger—able to bend iron without effort.
System Arbiter Lucius Cassel is investigating a statistical anomaly in the Scribal Quarter.
Guild Master Elara knows about the Arbiter's investigation and has warned Maret.
Theo has offered to help Maret if she is in trouble, but she has declined.
Maret can bend iron bars and steel with her bare hands.
Maret has decided to stay in Veridith and maintain her facade rather than flee.
The Arbiter will visit the guild tomorrow to interview senior scribes.

outline_json.title: The Arbiter's Inquiry

scenes.description: Cassel reviews the statistical anomaly report on his desk, noting the near-zero probability of the data corruption occurring naturally.

He accesses System logs for Maret Halsey, finding her physical activity records are sparse but her combat logs from a delve into the Ashfall Ruins show kills that require Strength far above her listed 3.
Cassel sends a mental command through his Arbiter badge, summoning Clerk Voss to bring the physical census records for the Scribal Quarter.
Clerk Voss arrives with the dusty ledgers; Cassel cross-references Maret's initial stats at age 14 with her current System file, confirming the numbers have never been officially updated.
Cassel dismisses Voss and contemplates the possibilities: a System error, deliberate manipulation by an external party, or a genuine anomaly in Maret herself.
He pulls up Theo Marlow's records, noting that the adventurer has delved with Maret multiple times and his logs corroborate her improbable combat contributions.

A search through the local gossip logs reveals a witness statement from a guild apprentice who claims Maret bent an iron bar during an argument, but no official System log of the event exists because no registered Arbiter or priest witnessed it.
Cassel weighs the evidence: the anomaly is real, and protocol dictates a formal summons for interrogation, though he knows it will put Maret on guard.
He drafts the summons document with precise legal language, listing the specific charges of statistical anomaly and log inconsistency, then seals it with his Arbiter sigil.
Cassel hands the sealed summons to Clerk Voss with orders to dispatch it immediately to the Scribal Guild
Alone again, Cassel stares at the brass lamp, wrestling with the unspoken implication: if Maret's stats are false, then the System itself may be flawed, and his entire life's work is built on a lie.

outline_json.purpose: Reveals Cassel's methodical investigation of the statistical anomaly, his growing certainty that Maret is the source, and his decision to issue a formal summons, ending with the summons being dispatched.

establishedFacts.fact: The data corruption in the Scribal Quarter records has a less than 0.001% probability of occurring naturally.
Maret's System logs show combat performance inconsistent with her listed Strength of 3.
A witness statement exists describing Maret bending an iron bar, but no official System log records the event.
Lucius Cassel decides to issue a formal summons for Maret Halsey.
The formal summons has been dispatched to the Scribal Guild.

outline_json.title: The Summons

scenes.description: Maret and Theo share a quiet meal at the Pious Rest, discussing a recent guild commission. The warm, bustling atmosphere is interrupted by the tavern's front door opening and a man in Arbiter's robes entering, scanning the room.

The Arbiter, Lucius Cassel, walks directly to their table and stops, his gaze fixed on Maret. He introduces himself formally, stating his title and purpose, then sits down without invitation, placing a leather folder on the table.

Cassel opens the folder and produces an official summons document, embossed with the Arbiter seal. He explains that Maret is required to attend an interrogation at the Arbiter Precinct to clarify statistical discrepancies in her System records.

Theo speaks up, asking what kind of discrepancies and why an Arbiter would handle a scribe's paperwork. Cassel turns to him with a cold, precise reply, stating that obstruction of an Arbiter's duty carries separate penalties, and Theo's involvement is not requested.

Maret reads the summons herself, noting the precise time and date: noon tomorrow at the Arbiter Precinct. The legal language leaves no room for ambiguity; failure to appear constitutes admission of guilt.

Maret attempts to stall, citing her duty to complete a critical commission for the Scribal Guild. Cassel counters that Arbiter authority supersedes guild regulations, and that the timetable is non-negotiable. The other patrons grow quiet, watching.

Cassel warns that if Maret refuses or fails to appear, he will escalate the matter to a warrant for arrest, with all city guards notified. He implies that fleeing would only confirm the anomaly's threat. The tension in the tavern thickens.

Maret internally weighs her options: compliance means exposing her secret to the System, possibly being taken for study; flight means abandoning her life and Theo, becoming a hunted fugitive. Neither path is safe.

She asks Cassel for until the next morning to arrange her guild affairs, hoping to buy time. Cassel agrees, but emphasizes that the interrogation must begin by noon—no extensions. He gives a final warning: the city guard has been notified to watch all exits from the Scribal Quarter.

Cassel stands, collects the folder, and leaves without another word. The tavern's noise slowly returns to normal, but Maret feels the eyes of some patrons lingering on her. Theo puts a hand on her arm, asking if she's okay.

Theo expresses his concern and offers to help, saying he can watch her back or act as a witness. Maret feels torn—she knows she can't explain the truth, but she appreciates his loyalty. She deflects weakly.

Alone with her thoughts after Theo leaves, Maret decides she will not flee. She will face the interrogation, but she will prepare—gather evidence, perhaps hide her true logs. A quiet resolve settles over her, tempered by fear. The chapter ends with her staring at the summons on the table.

outline_json.purpose: Confrontation between Maret and Cassel where he presents the summons for interrogation, forcing her to choose between compliance and flight, ending with the threat of escalation if she refuses.

character_system_awareness.perspective: Proud and loyal. He considers the Order the ultimate authority, above all other institutions.
Practical. He respects their records but considers them secondary to System logs.
Reverent. He sees the System as perfect and his role as its servant.
Fearful and resentful. She sees them as agents of oppression who enforce a flawed system.
Strategic. She uses the guild as cover and appreciates its administrative power, but she does not fully belong there.
Distrustful. She knows the System lied to her, so she sees it as an unreliable narrator rather than a divine arbiter.
Neutral respect. He sees them as distant authority figures, not relevant to his daily life.
Respectful. He knows they hold information power but doesn't envy them.
Accepting. He trusts his stats and the System's fairness, believing it reflects his own effort.

customs_json: Daily meditation on the System's Core Principles (a list of 12 rules).
A mandatory annual 'Log Review' where each Arbiter submits their own logs for peer audit.
Ritual purification (fasting and bathing in blessed water) before a major case.
cultures.description: An ascetic, disciplined order that sees itself as the System's sword and shield. They have distinct rituals, oaths, and a belief in absolute order. Arbiter Cassel embodies this culture: he is methodical, devout, and uncompromising.
cultures.name: Arbiter Order Culture
cultures.speech_influences: Arbiters speak in a formal, measured tone, often using legal phrases like 'in accordance with the Framework,' 'per the logged evidence,' and 'this is a matter of record.' They avoid emotional language. They always refer to individuals by their full formal name and class (e.g., 'Scribe Maret, Level 23').

system_views_json.sys_system: The absolute truth; any deviation is a threat.
system_views_json.sys_arbiter_order: The highest calling; they see themselves as the guardians of the Framework.
system_views_json.sys_scribal_guild: A necessary administrative body, but their records are only as reliable as their oversight.

taboos_json: Accepting bribes or favors from any citizen.
Displaying favoritism or leniency toward a specific class or person.
Using Arbiter authority without a proper case number (can lead to trial).
values_json: Justice through absolute adherence to the System's rules.
Selflessness: personal feelings must not interfere with duty.
Purity: Arbiters must not have any unresolved anomalies in their own logs.

customs_json: Morning rituals of sharpening quills and preparing materials.
Annual 'Ink Festival' to celebrate the founding of the guild.
Each new member receives a personalized inkwell as a rite of passage.
cultures.description: A guild culture of literacy and bureaucracy. They value precision, secrecy, and neutrality. Scribes often operate as information brokers. Maret is a Scribe; her knowledge has helped her hide her true stats.
cultures.name: Scribes
cultures.speech_influences: Scribes speak formally, with precise vocabulary and a tendency to qualify statements. They often use guild jargon like 'ink-stained' (tired from work) and 'quill-dry' (a period of inactivity). They rarely discuss personal stats, even among themselves.

system_views_json.sys_system: Viewed as a tool and a resource to be documented, not worshipped. Scribes are pragmatists.
system_views_json.sys_arbiter_order: Regarded with wary respect; they cooperate but maintain a professional distance.
system_views_json.sys_scribal_guild: Their own guild is the highest authority in their lives; they are fiercely loyal.

taboos_json: Destroying or falsifying a document intentionally (punishable by guild expulsion).
Sharing guild internal information with outsiders without permission.
Using combat skills inside a Scriptorium.
values_json: Neutrality in political conflicts; scribes serve all equally.
Protecting guild secrets (including their own internal records).

customs_json: Attending weekly 'System Sermons' at the Temple to hear updates and moral lessons.
Celebrating a child's Assessment day with feasting.
Forming parties for dungeon delves; each member expected to contribute according to class.
cultures.description: The general populace of Veridith, a mix of merchants, artisans, laborers, and low-level adventurers. They live under the System's hierarchy and accept it as natural. Most are law-abiding, focusing on daily survival and gradual advancement.
cultures.name: Veridith Citizens
cultures.speech_influences: People often begin conversations with a formal greeting including class and level, e.g., 'Greetings, Scribe Maret of Level 23.' They avoid direct discussion of others' stats. Slang includes 're-spec' for a rare class change and 'dump stat' for a very low attribute.

system_views_json.sys_system: Revered and accepted as divine. Most see it as the foundation of their world.
system_views_json.sys_arbiter_order: Feared and obeyed, but not loved. Seen as necessary but dangerous.
system_views_json.sys_scribal_guild: Respected as keepers of knowledge; somewhat feared for their ability to blacklist people.

taboos_json: Questioning the System's fairness openly (seen as heresy).
Consorting with known 'System cheaters' or exploiters.
Using unauthorized methods to alter stats (rare, but rumored).
values_json: Respecting the System's judgment: one's class is one's destiny.
Hard work and leveling as virtuous pursuits.
Community and mutual aid among people of similar level.

seed_json.premise: In a world governed by the System — a divine framework that assigns every person a class, tracks their statistics, and rewards growth with tangible power — a young scribe's stats have been lying since she received them at fourteen. Her Strength reads 3. She has bent iron bars with her hands. The System has never corrected the discrepancy. Now a System Arbiter has arrived in her city to investigate an anomaly — her anomaly.

characters.name: Maret
characters.description: Scribe, early 20s. Has lived with false stats for eight years by hiding what she can do. Uses her official Scribe class as cover — the administrative access it grants lets her avoid most physical situations. Smart, secretive, deeply uncertain whether the System is protecting her or preparing something. Has never told anyone her real numbers.

characters.name: Journeyman Theo
characters.description: A young adventurer in Maret's city. His stats are genuinely mediocre — he knows exactly what he is and is at peace with it. Befriended Maret two years ago because she's smarter than anyone in his party. Has noticed that she avoids combat situations in ways that don't match a Scribe's normal reasons. Doesn't push. Asks good questions.

characters.name: Arbiter Cassel
characters.description: System Arbiter, mid-40s. Investigates discrepancies between System records and observed reality. Genuinely believes in the System's justice — the Framework doesn't make mistakes, so anomalies must be corrected. His case file has Maret flagged as Threat Level: Unknown. He's not wrong to be cautious. He also doesn't know the System flagged her anomaly eight years ago and chose not to correct it.

constraints_json: Arbiters cannot modify class or change fundamental class features—only the System can.
They are bound by strict protocols; any deviation leads to immediate suspension and investigation.
They cannot use their authority for personal gain; doing so results in permanent removal and stat reduction.
world_systems.description: An elite order dedicated to enforcing the System's integrity. Arbiters are chosen from high-level individuals with a Justice-oriented class (e.g., Paladin, Inquisitor). They have authority to access all System logs, conduct investigations, and impose stat adjustments as punishment. They are feared and respected, but also distrusted—their power is unchecked.
manifestations_json: Arbiters wear black robes with silver trim and a badge that glows with System authority.
Their presence often makes people uncomfortable; civilians avoid eye contact.
They have the power to lock down an area for investigation, preventing entry/exit via System barriers.
world_systems.name: Arbiter Order
rules_json: Arbiters can compel any person to submit to a System Log Audit; refusal is considered a crime.
They can impose minor stat penalties (up to 10% of base) for violations, pending a review by the Temple.
Arbiters are immune to legal prosecution by civil authorities; they answer only to the Temple Council.
An Arbiter must have at least one assistant who is not an Arbiter (to prevent corruption).
vocabulary_json: Audit: A systematic check of a person's System logs.
Flag: A notification in the System that an anomaly has been detected.
Correction: An Arbiter-ordered stat adjustment to align with observed reality.
Threat Level: A rating from 1 (safe) to Unknown (potential existential risk).

constraints_json: Scribes cannot create System-consecrated documents—only the Temple can do that.
The guild's power is limited by the Temple and Arbiter Order; they cannot deny requests from Arbiters.
Forgery is possible (though hard to detect) and is a high crime punishable by stat reduction.
world_systems.description: A powerful guild that controls all administrative documentation, legal records, and public copying. Scribes are respected for their literacy and access to information, but considered low-level in combat. The guild has a monopoly on ink production and papermaking.
manifestations_json: Scribes wear a distinctive blue armband with a quill emblem.
Public documents (census, tax rolls, property deeds) are stored at guild halls and can be viewed for a fee.
Scribes often serve as notaries for contracts, especially between adventurer parties.
world_systems.name: Scribal Guild
rules_json: All legal documents must be copied by a licensed Scribe to be considered valid under System law.
Scribes have access to public System logs for document verification, but accessing private logs requires Arbiter authorization.
Guild membership is hereditary but can be earned through a series of tests; the class 'Scribe' is not required to join.
Scribes are prohibited from using combat skills within guild premises; violation leads to expulsion.
vocabulary_json: Quill-lock: A magical seal placed on documents that can only be undone by the original Scribe.
Ink-stain: A record of a 'defective' document that was improperly copied; can lead to fines.
Manuscript: A hand-copied book, often a skill codex or historical record.
Reading: A public event where a scribe reads aloud new laws or System updates.

constraints_json: The System cannot create new classes or change a person's class once assigned, except through a rare 'Class Change' quest (only 12 documented cases).
The System does not prevent actions but only records them—it offers no protection from harm.
Stat modification is extremely rare; most people never experience it outside of leveling gains.
The System cannot interpret intent, only record observable action.
world_systems.description: A divine framework that assigns every person a class, tracks their statistics (stats, level, skills), and grants rewards for growth. It manifests as a user interface visible only to the individual, with notifications, stat sheets, and quest logs. The System is omniscient in observation but does not interpret—it records all actions.
manifestations_json: Faint, translucent notifications appear in the field of view when events occur (level up, quest update, stat change).
The Assessment ceremony is public, with the System projecting the new class and stats onto a crystal for all to see.
Dungeons have clear entrance boundaries and a floating marker listing the recommended level range.
Trained users can access a full stat sheet by mentally commanding it; others perceive a visible aura based on innate Spirit stat.
world_systems.name: The System Framework
rules_json: At age 14, every person undergoes an Assessment where the System permanently assigns a class and initial stats.
Stats increase through leveling (gaining experience from kills and quests) and rare System rewards.
Skills are learned through class-specific training or leveling, with a cap on number of skills known (5 + level/10).
The System logs all actions with a timestamp and location; logs can be accessed by Arbiters and, with permission, by the individual.
vocabulary_json: Assessment: The ceremony at age 14 where class is assigned.
Stat sheet: The personal display of attributes (Strength, Dexterity, Constitution, Intelligence, Wisdom, Charisma, Spirit).
Leveling: Gaining experience through System-recognized actions (killing monsters, completing quests, crafting).
Quest: An objective issued by the System, often through a Quest Board (a crystal interface).
Arbiter: A System-enforcer authorized to access logs and impose penalties.
